next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

The lie stayed chalk-dry at the back of his mouth all the way home. Inside the flat he stripped the blanket off the sofa bed and snapped it once toward the window, stirring up stale sleep and the lemon spray from the pound shop that could not beat damp plaster or old frying oil. There were forty minutes until Eszter said she would be at the chicken shop on West Green Road. He shoved his Mercer tie into the drawer under the television stand, kicked Laci's spare work boots behind the armchair, and folded yesterday's shirt so its collar stains did not show. The room still looked like what it was: a front room rented as a bedroom because London could always find one more way to charge a man for standing upright indoors.

The buses made the glass shiver every few minutes. Somebody downstairs was frying something sweet with too much cinnamon. Somewhere in the building a child cried in short outraged bursts, stopped, started again. Beni stood in the middle of the room with the blanket in his hands and tried to decide whether he had made it better or only arranged the embarrassment into straighter lines.

At noon, in the Ashdown basement kitchen, Eszter had said it like an instruction.

"I'm finishing at six. There's a place near Seven Sisters that panics before closing and drops the chicken price. I'll get one. You buy potatoes and sour cream. Don't do anything stupid like light candles."

He had looked up from the slice of bread he was eating. "Why would I light candles?"

"Because you're nineteen and frightened."

"I'm not frightened."

"Then don't clean too hard ei...
open_hooks_to_carry: Make Jonas's studio feel materially different from the Ashdown house: messy, performative, and no less status-driven.
Show Nina's dependence on Beni growing because he solves problems without moral commentary.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...e had a point. Even the appliances in rich houses made their grievances known. They whined, clicked, clogged, leaked. They did not look straight through you while relying on you not to fail.

The phone in his pocket buzzed with Nina's name before either of them had to decide what the next question might be. He looked at the screen and felt the room tighten back into what it officially was.

"Go," Eszter said. Not unkindly. Already she was half up, collecting the bowl and mug together in one hand. "The palace is calling."

He stood. "Thank you. For lunch."

"For the food, yes. For the interrogation, no. That was free." She moved to the sink and ran hot water into the bowl. Over the sound she added, "Pick up Bea on time. If the school fines them, they'll probably take it from my wages out of principle."

"I will."

"I know."

The words followed him back through the corridor more closely than they should have.

At the service door he stopped long enough to wipe his hand on his trousers before calling her back. Nina answered without greeting.

"Bring the car round," she said. "We're going out."

"Yes, Mrs. Ashdown."

He put the phone away and stepped back into the mews. Rain drifted under the arch in a fine cold spray and got into his collar. The day had closed over again: keys, timings, mirrors, other people's urgency laid neatly over his next hours. But the kitchen heat was still in his hands, and when he thought about the hours ahead he caught himself measuring them against the next time he might have a reason to go back downstairs.

That was new enough to feel like trouble.
state_deltas_required: Deliver Nina's forgotten pills and catalogue to Jonas and return without giving Oscar a reason to investigate.
Beni lies for Nina for the first time, crossing from passive discretion into active complicity.
